comments.author: @1954WH
comments.text: Do some people live in boats, as, for instance, it still happens to be the case in the city of Amsterdam in Holland in The Netherlands?

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: That’s a really interesting comparison! While Amsterdam’s houseboats are a modern urban lifestyle choice with fully equipped floating homes, in South Sudan—especially in the vast Sudd wetland area—some indigenous communities (like the Nuer and Shiluk) do live along the Nile on temporary floating reed islands or traditional canoes called 'pirogues'. However, their way of life is centered around fishing and cattle herding in raw nature, purely for survival, rather than permanent urban floating houses. Thanks for bringing up such a fascinating perspective!

comments.author: @RealLifeExplorer.US26
comments.text: HSBC building installing crane "cannons" to block the Feng Shui bad vibes from Bank of China is peak Hong Kong drama!

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: That Feng Shui story is always so wild to think about! Thanks for watching and sharing your thoughts. See you in the next video!

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: Haha, absolutely! The level of passive-aggressive architectural warfare in Hong Kong is unmatched. Tying multi-million-dollar structural engineering directly to Feng Shui is the ultimate flex! 😂🏙️

comments.author: @RawLifeDocumentary.EN26
comments.text: A train system with a 99.9% on-time rate where a 5-minute delay is a "serious incident"? My city's subway system could never.

replies.author: @GlobeDiscovery-NY
replies.text: Thanks for sharing this perspective! The stark differences in public infrastructure and operational standards across different global cities always bring an interesting angle to these documentaries. Appreciate you tuning in and dropping your takeaway. Hope to still see your commentary on our future journeys!
